Portions of the tube that are in contact with bismuth will be coated with a 0.005-in. layer of tungsten to prevent attack of the stainless steel by the bismuth.

The remaining parts of this report describe: (1) experiments on the hydrodynamics of packed column operation, carried out in a mild-steel reductive extraction facility, (2) measurement of axial dispersion in packed columns in which immiscible fluids having large density differences are flowing countercurrently, (3) a simplified method for estimating the effect of axial dispersion on countercurrent column performance, (4) estimates of the effect of axial dispersion in packed column contactors used for MSBR processing, (5) measurements of axial dispersion coefficients in an open bubble column, (6) experiments related to the development of electrolytic cells for use with molten salt and bismuth, (7) the design and installation of the Flow Electrolytic Cell Facility, (8) the calibration of an orifice--head pot flowmeter for use with the Flow Electrolytic Cell Facility, (9) the development of an induction type of bismuth-salt interface detector, and (10) calculations regarding the removal of $\mathrm{ThF}_4$ from molten-salt streams by reductive extraction.
This work was carried out in the Chemical Technology Division during the period July through September 1969.

2.1 Hydrodynamic Run HR-4

The objectives of the fourth hydrodynamic experiment (Run HR-4) were: (1) to test the effectiveness of revisions in the salt overflow line, and (2) to investigate a new method for controlling the liquid level in the salt jackleg.
However, this run had to be terminated after only 5 min of operation because salt and bismuth were found to be leaking from the bismuth overflow line near the base of the column.
Examination of the 3/8-in.-diam mild steel tubing disclosed the presence of two holes - one about 1/8 in. in diam and the other essentially a crack.
The second crack-like hole was located about 2 in. above the first hole at the juncture of the tubing with a weld fillet, which attached the tube to the base of the column.
wall thickness of the section that had been in service for the longest period of time ( $>500 \, \text{hr}$ ) had been reduced from 0.058 in. to as little as 0.010 in. in some locations.
A second failure occurred in the 3/8-in. steel transfer line connecting the bismuth receiver and the treatment vessel; however, it did not result in the loss of salt or bismuth.
Shortly after this line was heated to about $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in order to melt a suspected salt plug, the resistance heater grounded to the tubing and burned a hole in the tubing.

section: 2.2 Treatment of Bismuth and Salt with $\mathsf{H}_2$ -HF
text: The flow of the H<sub>2</sub>-HF mixture was interrupted twice when a small amount of material (salt-like in appearance) collected in the off-gas line.
2.3 Hydrodynamic Run HR-5
The system modifications that were made prior to Run HR-4 to improve the control of the salt and bismuth flow rates were shown to be effective during Run HR-5.
The automatic level control for the salt jackleg held the liquid level constant to within $\pm 0.05$ in.
Run HR-5 began with a 28-min period of bismuth flow only, during which the flow rate was stable at 80 ml/min (nominal).
Salt flow was then started, and about 20 min of operation was required for the salt flow rate to become steady.
However, approximately 15 min of this time period was required to fill the salt jackleg.
During Run HR-5, stable flows of salt and bismuth were obtained at six sets of flow rates, as shown in Table 1.
Table 1 also shows the apparent bismuth holdup in the column during the periods of steady flow.
This is shown most strikingly by a comparison of time intervals 4 and 5, during which the apparent holdup failed to change even though $V_{c}$ was doubled.

A small stream of water (flow rate, approximately $1\mathrm{ml} / \mathrm{min}$ ) is withdrawn from the column, circulated through a cell containing a light source and a photocell, and then returned to the column at the same elevation.
The photocells are cylindrical in shape and have a $1/2$ -in. inside diameter.
The light path, $1/2$ in. in length, lies along the axis of the cell.
The tracer concentration in each photocell is observed until steady state is reached; with a 4-ft column, this normally takes 2 to 4 hr.

text: The point of injection was 11-7/8 in. above the bottom of the column.
Each of the pumps was fabricated from Lucite and had a 0.465-in.-long, 2-in.-diam pump body.
The chamber in which the impeller operated consisted of a 15/16-in.-diam, 3/8-in.-deep hole drilled along the axis of the pump body.
The chamber was sealed by a 3/8-in.-thick, 1-in.-diam Lucite seal plate that was machined to a diameter of 15/16 in. over a distance of 1/8 in.
The inlet to the pump consisted of a 1/8-in.-diam hole drilled on the axis of the seal plate.
The outer 1/4 in. of the inlet was drilled and tapped to receive a 1/8-in.-ID, 1-in.-long section of stainless steel tubing.
The pump outlet consisted of a 1/8-in.-diam hole through the pump body; the outlet was tangential to the surface of the pump chamber.
The outlet was also provided with a 1-in. section of 1/8-in.-diam stainless steel tubing.
The pump impeller consisted of a 3/4-in.-long, Teflon-coated magnet, which was magnetically coupled to the pump drive system.
The cell body, fabricated from Lucite, was 1 in. in diameter and 1.75 in. long.
A 0.438-in.-diam hole was drilled from both ends of the cell body in such a manner that a 1/8-in.-thick partition, 0.5 in. from one end of the cell body, was formed.
The cell compartment was formed by gluing a 0.438-in.-diam, 1/8-in.-thick Lucite disk (shaded area) inside the drilled hole, 0.5 in. from the other end of the cell body.
The light source for the photocell was a GE 253X bulb, and the transmittance of the cell was measured by a Clairex 707L photoresistor, which has maximum sensitivity at a wavelength of 6150 Å.

To collimate the light passing through the cell, the light source and the photoresistor were mounted inside 0.436-in.-OD, 6-in.-long brass tubes that were inserted into the outer compartments of the cell.

16, consisted of a permanent iron magnet firmly attached to an iron rod, a chuck employed to adjust the height of the magnet above the pump, and a sprocket wheel bolted to the iron shaft that supported the chuck.
The electrical signal from the photoresistor is amplified by a transistorized operational amplifier circuit and displayed on a Honeywell Brown recorder (voltage range, -0.5 to $10.5\mathrm{mV}$ ).
The 20 electrical circuits for the cells were connected to the recorder by two 10-position selector switches that allowed the desired cell reading to be displayed.

In calibrating the photocells, it was necessary (1) to determine the maximum tracer concentration for which the linear approximation between the fraction of incident light absorbed (absorbance) and the tracer concentration would be valid, and (2) to confirm the assumption of a linear relationship between the cell reading and the tracer concentration.
Figure 20 shows results which verify that behavior corresponding to Beer's law was observed.
In this examination, a Beckman infrared spectrophotometer and quartz cells (1 cm in length) were used.
The maximum sensitivity of the photoresistor occurred at a wavelength of 6150 Å.
of $0.045 \underline{\mathbf{M}}$ resulted in an absorbance of 0.13.

The absorbance based on the linear approximation was calculated by using this concentration and the molar extinction coefficient at $6150 \text{ Å}$ ; the error in absorbance caused by the linear approximation was only $6\%$ of the actual absorbance.
The tracer concentration of $0.045 \underline{\mathbf{M}}$ was chosen as the upper limit for the linear approximation.
The 20 photocells were calibrated initially at tracer concentrations below the experimentally determined concentration limit by the following procedure.
Then the pumps were activated, and their pumping rates were adjusted to $0.12\mathrm{cm}^3/\mathrm{sec}$ .
A 15-min period was allowed for the cells to reach thermal equilibrium.
Next, the bottom cell was disconnected from the column and filled with a $0.041\mathrm{M}\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{NO}_3)_2$ solution.
The amplifier feedback of the cell was adjusted so that the cell reading on the recorder was $95\%$ of full scale.
Air flow through the column was then started at a volumetric flow rate of $4.30\mathrm{cm}^3/\mathrm{sec}$ .
The infusion pump was started, and the tracer flow rate was adjusted to $0.407\mathrm{cm}^3/\mathrm{sec}$ .
Injection of tracer was terminated when the bottom cell reading on the recorder was $80\%$ of full scale.
A uniform tracer concentration was assumed to be present in the column when the top cell reading maintained a constant value for $15\mathrm{min}$ .
The amplifier feedback of the 19 cells was then adjusted so that all cell readings were equal to the reading of the bottom cell.

Preparation of the tracer involved dissolving $230\mathrm{g}$ of laboratory-grade $\mathrm{Cu(NO_3)_2} \cdot 3\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ crystals in a 2-liter volumetric flask half-filled with distilled water, adding $20\mathrm{ml}$ of concentrated nitric acid, and diluting to volume with distilled water.
The $\mathrm{Cu(NO_3)_2}$ concentration was approximately $0.48\mathrm{M}$ .
Distilled water was passed through the column with the cell pumps operating at the rate of $0.12\mathrm{cm}^3/$ sec.
A period of approximately 30 min was allowed to ensure complete removal of tracer from previous runs and to obtain thermal equilibrium in the cells.

Air flow was started through the column at a volumetric flow rate of 4.5 liters/min, the water flow was terminated, and tracer was injected into the column by operating the infusion pump at the rate of $0.41\mathrm{cm}^3/$ sec.
The injection of tracer was stopped when the recorder reading of the bottom cell reached $80\%$ of full scale, and the tracer solution was allowed to mix in the column.
A uniform tracer concentration was assumed to be present when the reading of the top cell maintained a constant value for 10 min.
Steady state was assumed to have been reached when the reading for the top cell maintained a constant value for 10 min.
Water was fed through the column for 30 min with the pumps activated in order to remove the tracer from the cells.
If the final value for a particular cell deviated by more than $5\%$ from the initial value, data from that particular cell were discarded.

The magnitude of this effect was checked by comparing axial dispersion coefficients measured at two pumping rates (0.18 cm³/sec and 0.12 cm³/sec) but a fixed superficial liquid velocity (0.13 cm/sec) and gas flow rate (8.2 cm³/sec).

The measured dispersion coefficients were 28.6 cm²/sec and 28.4 cm²/sec for pumping rates of 0.12 cm³/sec and 0.18 cm³/sec, respectively; the deviation of these coefficients from their average was less than 1%.

This effect must be considered since the lower tracer feed rate of $0.081~\mathrm{cm}^3/\mathrm{sec}$ was used at the superficial liquid velocity of $0.08~\mathrm{cm/sec}$ in order to avoid exceeding the tracer concentration limit at the bottom of the column.

The dispersion coefficients were 28.4 $\mathrm{cm}^2/\mathrm{sec}$ and $26.7~\mathrm{cm}^2/\mathrm{sec}$ for tracer feed rates of $0.407~\mathrm{cm}^3/\mathrm{sec}$ and $0.163~\mathrm{cm}^3/\mathrm{sec}$ , respectively; the deviation of each of these values from their average value was less than $4\%$ .

section: 7. ELECTROLYTIC CELL DEVELOPMENT: STATIC CELL EXPERIMENTS
text: The objectives were: (1) determination of conditions under which a protective frozen salt film can be maintained on electrode dividers in the presence of high heat generation in the salt, and (2) identification of a black material which has formed in the salt during all static-cell experiments carried out to date.

section: 7.2 Experiment Using Lead-Acid Storage Batteries for Power Supply
text: The ac component has been suspected to cause the formation of a dark-colored material, which has been repeatedly observed in the salt in all static-cell experiments to date, since it has been reported<sup>14</sup> that sols of electrode metals can be produced in molten salts by use of alternating current.

section: 7.2 Experiment Using Lead-Acid Storage Batteries for Power Supply
text: The cell used for this experiment was made from a 4-in.-diam quartz tube and contained a 3-in.-high quartz divider, which separated two hemicylindrical bismuth electrodes having a surface area of about $30~\mathrm{cm}^2$ each.

section: 7.2 Experiment Using Lead-Acid Storage Batteries for Power Supply
text: The cell contained bismuth that had been purified by hydrogen sparging and molten salt (66-34 mole % LiF- $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ ) that had been sparged with hydrogen to remove residual HF.

section: 7.2 Experiment Using Lead-Acid Storage Batteries for Power Supply
text: The power supply consisted of five 6-V lead-acid storage batteries, each of which was rated at 200 A-hr.

section: 8. DESIGN AND INSTALLATION OF THE FLOW ELECTROLYTIC CELL FACILITY
text: A facility for continuously circulating molten salt and bismuth through electrolytic cells at temperatures up to $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is being installed in Bldg.

section: 8. DESIGN AND INSTALLATION OF THE FLOW ELECTROLYTIC CELL FACILITY
text: The equipment consists of a 16-in.-diam vessel that will contain the cell to be tested, a mixer-settler tank in which the salt and bismuth streams from the cell will be equilibrated, gas-lift pumps and orifice--head pot flowmeters for circulating and metering the streams to the cell, and a vessel containing a graphite crucible for purifying the salt and bismuth.

section: 8. DESIGN AND INSTALLATION OF THE FLOW ELECTROLYTIC CELL FACILITY
text: Also provided are a gas supply station for metering HF, hydrogen, argon, and nitrogen; a system for disposing of unreacted HF; a 750-A dc power supply; and instrumentation for recording pressures, temperatures, current, and voltage.
Bismuth and salt are circulated by gas-lift pumps at nominal flow rates of 0.5 and 0.25 gpm,
Figures 52 and 53 show the equipment before thermal insulation was applied, while Fig.
54 shows it after installation in a walk-in hood in Bldg.
52) is a 16-in.-diam, 21-in.-high vessel constructed of 1/4-in.-thick stainless steel.
The usable space is 15-1/2 in. in diameter and 16 in. deep.
The nominal operating temperature is $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Two sight glasses, placed $180^{\circ}$ apart in the vessel wall, allow visual observation of the cell.
55) is a carbon-steel horizontal tank, 8 in. in diameter with dishes heads, and has a total capacity of about 960 in.<sup>3</sup>.
The gas-liquid mixtures from the gas-lift pumps discharge into two 4-in.-diam by 7-in.-long carbon-steel disengagement vessels (Fig.
52), which separate the gas and the liquid phases as well as damp surges in the liquid flow rate to the cell vessel.
57) is divided into two concentric compartments.
The treatment vessel is a 16-in.-OD by 17-1/2-in.-high vessel constructed of 1/4-in.-thick 304 type stainless steel.
The transfer line from the treatment vessel contains a filter that consists of a 4-in.-diam by 1/8-in.-thick porous molybdenum disk having a mean pore diameter of about $30\mu$ .

The vessel, which will contain both the bismuth cathode pool and the electrolyte pool, is fabricated of sections of 5-in. and 8-in. sched 40 carbon-steel pipe.
The inlet reservoirs for the bismuth and the salt streams, made of 1-in. sched 40 pipe, are connected to the main cell vessel by 1-in.-long sections of $3/4$ -in. tubing.
The salt and the metal levels are set by the height of a vented $1/2$ -in.-diam overflow line for each stream.

The dc power supply has an output of 0 to $24\mathrm{V}$ , a maximum current of 750 A, and a $4.2\%$ rms ac component.

(Or, if desired, it can be controlled manually.) The automatic voltage control will maintain constant voltage within $\pm 2\%$ in the 0- to 24-V range; the automatic current control will maintain constant current within $\pm 2\%$ in the range 75 to 750 A.

The objectives of the present studies are twofold: (1) to verify the applicability of this relation to the orifice--head pot flowmeters, and (2) to determine the orifice coefficient.
(24) by using data obtained during steady flow, or from a more convenient relation that was derived for use with transient flow.
Q = \frac {d \ell}{d t} A _ {h}, \tag {25}
Q = - \frac {A _ {h}}{\rho g} \frac {d \Delta P}{d t}. \tag {26}
\frac {d \Delta P}{\sqrt {\Delta P}} = - \frac {C _ {d} A g}{A _ {h}} \cdot 2 \rho d t. \tag {27}
\Delta P = (\Delta P) _ {\text {i n i t i a l}} \text {a t} t = 0,
\Delta \vec {P} = - \frac {\mathrm {C} _ {\mathrm {d}} \mathrm {A g}}{2 \mathrm {A} _ {\mathrm {h}}} \sqrt {2} \vec {\rho} t + (\Delta P) _ {\text {i n i t i a l}}. \tag {28}
Thus, a plot of $\sqrt[4]{\Delta P}$ vs $t$ should yield a straight line having a slope of $(-C_d A_g \sqrt[4]{2\rho / 2A_h})$ .

A modified version of a liquid-level induction probe developed at Argonne National Laboratory<sup>15</sup> has been built, and a study of this probe is presently under way.
66, consists of a 12-in.-long bifilar winding of 30-gage platinum wire.
The platinum wire is wound in grooves, which have been machined into the surface of a tubular lavite form having an outside diameter of 15/16 in. and an inside diameter of 9/16 in.
These grooves are 0.015 in. wide, 0.015 in. deep with a round bottom, and are separated by 0.035 in. of lavite.
Protective collars, 1 in. wide by 1-7/16 in. in diameter, are located at each end of the coil.
The input to the primary coil is supplied by a Wavetek Function Generator Model 110** and is typically $20\mathrm{kc}$ at $2\mathrm{V}$ .
The output of the secondary coil is amplified to about $6\mathrm{V}$ , rectified, and filtered.
The residual current is suppressed to eliminate the zero-level signal, and the differential voltage (e.g., $10\mathrm{mV}$ dc) is amplified and fed to a strip-chart recorder.
The interface detector will be mounted for testing on a seamless type 316 stainless steel tube having a 0.065-in. wall and an outside diameter of 0.500 in.
The portion of the inside length of the tube that will be in contact with liquid bismuth is being coated with a 0.005-in. layer of tungsten.

Similarly, the portion of the outside diameter of the tube that will be located inside the liquid bismuth reservoir will be coated with a 0.005-in. layer of tungsten to preclude the possibility of a reaction taking place between the bismuth and the stainless steel.
